Card (1995) — Design Diagnostics & Weight Stability

Why is the Effective Sample Size so Low? Propensity Scores, Overlap, and Weak Instrument

Alexander Unger

09 June 2026

library(haven)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(AER)
library(sandwich)
library(lmtest)
library(scales)

# Shared kappa toolkit (logit_mle, get_cbps_p, kappa_outcome_weights,
# kappa_weights, weight_diag, ...). Nothing estimation-related is defined here.
source("functions_all.R")

# Consistent palette
col_z1   <- "#2c7bb6"   # Z = 1 (near college)
col_z0   <- "#d7191c"   # Z = 0 (far from college)
col_grey <- "#4d4d4d"
col_card <- "#2166ac"
col_kit  <- "#984ea3"
data_path <- "/Users/alexung/Desktop/master_thesis/code/"
card      <- read_dta(file.path(data_path, "card.dta"))
card      <- card[!is.na(card$wage) & !is.na(card$educ), ]

# Treatments, instrument, covariates
card$somecol <- as.integer(card$educ > 12)    # D1: some college
card$educ16  <- as.integer(card$educ >= 16)   # D2: college completion
card$Z       <- as.integer(card$nearc4)       # Z: grew up near 4-yr college
if (!"expersq" %in% names(card)) card$expersq <- card$exper^2

# Propensity scores p(X) = Pr(Z = 1 | X) under both specs
X_card <- model.matrix(~ exper + expersq + black + south + smsa + smsa66 +
                          reg661+reg662+reg663+reg664+reg665+reg666+reg667+reg668, card)
X_kit  <- model.matrix(~ black + south + smsa + smsa66 + south66, card)

card$p_card <- logit_mle(card$Z, X_card)
card$p_kit  <- logit_mle(card$Z, X_kit)

cat(sprintf("N = %d | Pr(Z=1) = %.3f | somecol = %.3f | educ16 = %.3f\n",
            nrow(card), mean(card$Z), mean(card$somecol), mean(card$educ16)))
## N = 3010 | Pr(Z=1) = 0.682 | somecol = 0.505 | educ16 = 0.271

1. The Puzzle

The finding to explain. In the Card application the effective sample size (ESS = \(1/\sum_i \omega_i^2\)) of the kappa weights collapses to single digits — for the Kitagawa specification it falls near 1 — even though \(N = 3{,}010\). This notebook diagnoses why, working through three candidate explanations: (1) a weak first stage, (2) propensity-score concentration / non-overlap, and (3) sign-reversing observations (high \(\hat p(X)\) yet \(Z=0\) and treated, or the reverse) that destabilize the kappa weights.

# Quick ESS preview across both specs and both treatments
ess_one <- function(D, p) {
  w <- kappa_outcome_weights(card$Z, D, p)$w_u
  1 / sum(w^2)
}
ess_tab <- data.frame(
  Specification = c("Card", "Kitagawa"),
  `somecol (ESS)` = c(ess_one(card$somecol, card$p_card),
                      ess_one(card$somecol, card$p_kit)),
  `educ16 (ESS)`  = c(ess_one(card$educ16,  card$p_card),
                      ess_one(card$educ16,  card$p_kit)),
  check.names = FALSE
)
ess_tab[,2:3] <- round(ess_tab[,2:3], 1)

ess_tab %>%
  kbl(caption = "Effective sample size (τ_ml_u weights) — the puzzle in one table",
      align = c("l","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = FALSE, font_size = 14) %>%
  column_spec(2:3, bold = TRUE, color = "#d7191c") %>%
  footnote(general = sprintf("Out of N = %d observations. ESS this low means a handful of observations carry essentially all the weight.", nrow(card)))
Effective sample size (τ_ml_u weights) — the puzzle in one table
Specification somecol (ESS) educ16 (ESS)
Card 3.8 1.1
Kitagawa 2.9 0.9
Note:
Out of N = 3010 observations. ESS this low means a handful of observations carry essentially all the weight.

2. First Stage: Is the Instrument Weak?

Why this matters. The kappa weights divide by the conditional first stage. A weak instrument means \(\Pr(D=1\mid Z=1,X) - \Pr(D=1\mid Z=0,X)\) is small for many cells; dividing by a near-zero first stage inflates weights and destroys ESS. Card’s instrument (college proximity) is known to be weaker than the Vietnam draft lottery.

fs_F <- function(formula, data) {
  fit <- lm(formula, data = data)
  ct  <- coeftest(fit, vcov = vcovHC(fit, type = "HC1"))
  c(coef = unname(ct["Z","Estimate"]),
    se   = unname(ct["Z","Std. Error"]),
    F    = unname((ct["Z","Estimate"]/ct["Z","Std. Error"])^2))
}

# First stage for both treatments and both specs
fs_rows <- rbind(
  data.frame(Treatment="somecol", Spec="Card",
             t(fs_F(somecol ~ Z + exper+expersq+black+south+smsa+smsa66+
                      reg661+reg662+reg663+reg664+reg665+reg666+reg667+reg668, card))),
  data.frame(Treatment="somecol", Spec="Kitagawa",
             t(fs_F(somecol ~ Z + black+south+smsa+smsa66+south66, card))),
  data.frame(Treatment="educ16", Spec="Card",
             t(fs_F(educ16 ~ Z + exper+expersq+black+south+smsa+smsa66+
                      reg661+reg662+reg663+reg664+reg665+reg666+reg667+reg668, card))),
  data.frame(Treatment="educ16", Spec="Kitagawa",
             t(fs_F(educ16 ~ Z + black+south+smsa+smsa66+south66, card)))
)
fs_rows$coef <- round(fs_rows$coef, 4)
fs_rows$se   <- round(fs_rows$se, 4)
fs_rows$F    <- round(fs_rows$F, 1)
fs_rows$`Stock-Yogo (>10)?` <- ifelse(fs_rows$F >= 10, "✓ strong", "✗ WEAK")

fs_rows %>%
  kbl(caption = "First-stage strength: effect of college proximity (Z) on each treatment",
      col.names = c("Treatment","Spec","First stage δ̂","SE","F-stat","Stock-Yogo (>10)?"),
      align = c("l","l","r","r","r","c")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) %>%
  row_spec(which(fs_rows$F < 10), background = "#fdeaea", bold = TRUE) %>%
  footnote(general = "HC1-robust first-stage F on the instrument. Compare to Vietnam, where F was far above 10. A weak first stage here is the first driver of weight instability.")
First-stage strength: effect of college proximity (Z) on each treatment
Treatment Spec First stage δ̂ SE F-stat Stock-Yogo (>10)?
somecol Card 0.0636 0.0180 12.5 ✓ strong
somecol Kitagawa 0.0653 0.0218 9.0 ✗ WEAK
educ16 Card 0.0302 0.0162 3.5 ✗ WEAK
educ16 Kitagawa 0.0379 0.0191 3.9 ✗ WEAK
Note:
HC1-robust first-stage F on the instrument. Compare to Vietnam, where F was far above 10. A weak first stage here is the first driver of weight instability. 
# First-stage gap: D-rate by Z, for somecol
fs_gap <- card %>%
  summarise(
    `Z = 1 (near college)` = mean(somecol[Z == 1]),
    `Z = 0 (far)`          = mean(somecol[Z == 0])
  )
gap_some <- fs_gap$`Z = 1 (near college)` - fs_gap$`Z = 0 (far)`

fs_gap_e <- card %>%
  summarise(
    `Z = 1 (near college)` = mean(educ16[Z == 1]),
    `Z = 0 (far)`          = mean(educ16[Z == 0])
  )
gap_educ <- fs_gap_e$`Z = 1 (near college)` - fs_gap_e$`Z = 0 (far)`

fs_plot_df <- data.frame(
  Treatment = rep(c("somecol\n(educ > 12)", "educ16\n(educ ≥ 16)"), each = 2),
  Z = rep(c("Z = 1 (near college)", "Z = 0 (far)"), 2),
  rate = c(fs_gap$`Z = 1 (near college)`, fs_gap$`Z = 0 (far)`,
           fs_gap_e$`Z = 1 (near college)`, fs_gap_e$`Z = 0 (far)`)
)

ggplot(fs_plot_df, aes(x = Treatment, y = rate, fill = Z)) +
  geom_col(position = position_dodge(0.7), width = 0.6, alpha = 0.9) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", 100*rate)),
            position = position_dodge(0.7), vjust = -0.4, size = 3.4) +
  annotate("text", x = 1, y = 0.55,
           label = sprintf("gap = %.3f", gap_educ), size = 3.5, colour = col_grey) +
  annotate("text", x = 2, y = 0.55,
           label = sprintf("gap = %.3f", gap_some), size = 3.5, colour = col_grey) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Z = 1 (near college)" = col_z1, "Z = 0 (far)" = col_z0)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1), limits = c(0, 0.62)) +
  labs(title = "First stage: treatment rates by college proximity",
       subtitle = "The gap between bars is the unconditional first stage. Small gaps = weak instrument.",
       x = NULL, y = "Treatment rate", fill = NULL,
       caption = "Card (1995), N = 3,010.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 10),
        plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 9))

3. Propensity Score: Concentration & Overlap

Why this matters. The kappa weights contain \(1/p(X)\) and \(1/(1-p(X))\) terms. Observations with \(\hat p(X)\) near 0 or 1 get enormous weights. Unlike Vietnam (where \(\hat p\) was tightly clustered around 0.46 by design), the richer Card and Kitagawa covariate sets can push \(\hat p(X)\) toward the boundaries — directly inflating weights.

Summary Statistics

ps_summary <- function(p, name) {
  data.frame(Specification = name,
             Min = min(p), P05 = quantile(p,0.05), P25 = quantile(p,0.25),
             Mean = mean(p), P75 = quantile(p,0.75), P95 = quantile(p,0.95),
             Max = max(p), SD = sd(p),
             `near 0/1` = mean(p < 0.05 | p > 0.95),
             check.names = FALSE)
}
ps_tab <- bind_rows(ps_summary(card$p_card, "Card (14 covariates)"),
                    ps_summary(card$p_kit,  "Kitagawa (5 covariates)"))
ps_tab[,2:10] <- lapply(ps_tab[,2:10], function(x) round(x, 4))

ps_tab %>%
  kbl(caption = "Propensity score p̂(X) = Pr(Z=1|X): summary by specification",
      align = c("l", rep("r", 9))) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 12.5) %>%
  column_spec(9, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(10, bold = TRUE, color = "#d7191c") %>%
  footnote(general = "Compare SD and the 'near 0/1' share against Vietnam (SD ≈ 0.10, essentially nothing near the boundaries). Higher dispersion here is the second driver of low ESS.")
Propensity score p̂(X) = Pr(Z=1|X): summary by specification
Specification Min P05 P25 Mean P75 P95 Max SD near 0/1
5%…1 Card (14 covariates) 0.1724 0.2459 0.4376 0.6821 0.8726 0.9341 0.9517 0.2356 0.0056
5%…2 Kitagawa (5 covariates) 0.2532 0.2532 0.4554 0.6821 0.8783 0.8783 0.9067 0.2280 0.0000
Note:
Compare SD and the ‘near 0/1’ share against Vietnam (SD ≈ 0.10, essentially nothing near the boundaries). Higher dispersion here is the second driver of low ESS.

Distribution by Instrument Status

ps_long <- card %>%
  select(p_card, p_kit, Z) %>%
  pivot_longer(c(p_card, p_kit), names_to = "spec", values_to = "p_hat") %>%
  mutate(Specification = factor(spec,
                                levels = c("p_card", "p_kit"),
                                labels = c("Card spec", "Kitagawa spec")),
         Zlab = factor(ifelse(Z == 1, "Z = 1 (near college)", "Z = 0 (far)"),
                       levels = c("Z = 1 (near college)", "Z = 0 (far)")))

ggplot(ps_long, aes(x = p_hat, fill = Zlab)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 40, alpha = 0.7, colour = "white", position = "identity") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = mean(card$Z), linetype = "dashed", colour = col_grey) +
  facet_wrap(~ Specification, ncol = 1, scales = "free_y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Z = 1 (near college)" = col_z1, "Z = 0 (far)" = col_z0)) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Propensity score distribution by instrument status",
       subtitle = "Overlap requires both colours present at every p̂. Gaps at the tails = overlap violations → exploding weights.",
       x = expression(hat(p)(X) == Pr(Z == 1 ~ "|" ~ X)), y = "Count", fill = NULL,
       caption = "Dashed line = pooled Pr(Z=1).") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9.5),
        plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 9))

What to look for. Where the red (Z=0) bars vanish at high \(\hat p\), or blue (Z=1) bars vanish at low \(\hat p\), the inverse-probability terms \(Z/p\) and \((1-Z)/(1-p)\) have no counterpart to balance against — those observations receive extreme weights. The Kitagawa panel, with only 5 binary covariates, produces discrete \(\hat p\) clumps that are especially prone to this.

4. Where Do the Weights Explode?

The mechanism. The kappa weight for the normalized estimator is built from \(Z/p - (1-Z)/(1-p)\) scaled by the first stage. Plotting each observation’s absolute weight against its \(\hat p(X)\) shows exactly which observations dominate — and confirms whether it is the tails of \(\hat p\), the sign-reversing cells, or both.

# Build per-observation weights for the worst cell (Kitagawa, the low-ESS one)
make_wdf <- function(D, p, spec, treat) {
  w <- kappa_outcome_weights(card$Z, D, p)$w_u
  data.frame(p_hat = p, w = w, abs_w = abs(w),
             Z = card$Z, D = D,
             Spec = spec, Treat = treat)
}
wdf <- bind_rows(
  make_wdf(card$somecol, card$p_kit,  "Kitagawa", "somecol"),
  make_wdf(card$somecol, card$p_card, "Card",     "somecol")
)
wdf$cell <- paste(wdf$Spec, "spec")
wdf$status <- factor(
  ifelse(wdf$Z == 1 & wdf$D == 1, "Z=1, treated",
  ifelse(wdf$Z == 1 & wdf$D == 0, "Z=1, untreated",
  ifelse(wdf$Z == 0 & wdf$D == 1, "Z=0, treated",
                                  "Z=0, untreated"))),
  levels = c("Z=1, treated","Z=1, untreated","Z=0, treated","Z=0, untreated"))

ggplot(wdf, aes(x = p_hat, y = abs_w, colour = status)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.45, size = 1.1) +
  facet_wrap(~ cell, ncol = 2, scales = "free") +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("Z=1, treated"   = "#2c7bb6",
                                 "Z=1, untreated" = "#abd9e9",
                                 "Z=0, treated"   = "#d7191c",
                                 "Z=0, untreated" = "#fdae61")) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format(accuracy = 1)) +
  labs(title = "Absolute outcome weight vs. propensity score (somecol)",
       subtitle = "Each point is one observation. Points high on the y-axis dominate the estimate and collapse the ESS.",
       x = expression(hat(p)(X)), y = expression("|" ~ omega[i] ~ "|"),
       colour = NULL,
       caption = "Watch for: 'Z=0, treated' at high p̂ and 'Z=1, untreated' at low p̂ — these sign-reversing cells generate the largest weights.") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom", strip.text = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(colour = "grey40", size = 9.5),
        plot.caption = element_text(colour = "grey50", size = 8.8))

The Top-Weighted Observations

# How concentrated? Show how much total weight the top-k observations carry.
conc_one <- function(D, p, spec) {
  w  <- kappa_outcome_weights(card$Z, D, p)$w_u
  aw <- sort(abs(w), decreasing = TRUE)
  tot <- sum(aw)
  data.frame(Spec = spec,
             `Top 1 (%)`  = round(100 * aw[1] / tot, 1),
             `Top 5 (%)`  = round(100 * sum(aw[1:5]) / tot, 1),
             `Top 10 (%)` = round(100 * sum(aw[1:10]) / tot, 1),
             `Top 25 (%)` = round(100 * sum(aw[1:25]) / tot, 1),
             ESS = round(1 / sum(w^2), 1),
             check.names = FALSE)
}
conc_tab <- bind_rows(
  conc_one(card$somecol, card$p_card, "Card / somecol"),
  conc_one(card$somecol, card$p_kit,  "Kitagawa / somecol"),
  conc_one(card$educ16,  card$p_card, "Card / educ16"),
  conc_one(card$educ16,  card$p_kit,  "Kitagawa / educ16")
)

conc_tab %>%
  kbl(caption = "Weight concentration: share of total |ω| carried by the most extreme observations",
      align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) %>%
  column_spec(6, bold = TRUE, color = "#d7191c") %>%
  footnote(general = "If a handful of observations carry most of the weight, the estimator is effectively using only those points — that is what the low ESS reports numerically.")
Weight concentration: share of total |ω| carried by the most extreme observations
Spec Top 1 (%) Top 5 (%) Top 10 (%) Top 25 (%) ESS
Card / somecol 0.3 1.3 2.5 6.2 3.8
Kitagawa / somecol 0.2 0.9 1.8 3.9 2.9
Card / educ16 0.3 1.3 2.5 6.2 1.1
Kitagawa / educ16 0.2 0.9 1.8 3.9 0.9
Note:
If a handful of observations carry most of the weight, the estimator is effectively using only those points — that is what the low ESS reports numerically.

5. Overlap Diagnosis by Treatment Cell

Putting it together. The cross-tabulation of \(Z\) × \(D\) within propensity-score bins shows where compliers actually live and where cells are empty or near-empty. Empty diagonal cells (e.g. high-\(\hat p\) but no \(Z=0\) controls) are the formal overlap violations.

overlap_by_bin <- function(D, p, spec) {
  bin <- cut(p, breaks = quantile(p, seq(0,1,0.2)), include.lowest = TRUE,
             labels = c("Q1 (low p̂)","Q2","Q3","Q4","Q5 (high p̂)"))
  out <- card %>%
    mutate(bin = bin, D = D) %>%
    group_by(bin) %>%
    summarise(n = n(),
              `n(Z=1)` = sum(Z == 1),
              `n(Z=0)` = sum(Z == 0),
              `Pr(D|Z=1)` = round(mean(D[Z==1]), 3),
              `Pr(D|Z=0)` = round(ifelse(sum(Z==0)>0, mean(D[Z==0]), NA), 3),
              `first stage` = round(mean(D[Z==1]) - ifelse(sum(Z==0)>0, mean(D[Z==0]), NA), 3),
              .groups = "drop")
  out$Spec <- spec
  out
}
ov_kit <- overlap_by_bin(card$somecol, card$p_kit, "Kitagawa")

ov_kit %>%
  select(bin, n, `n(Z=1)`, `n(Z=0)`, `Pr(D|Z=1)`, `Pr(D|Z=0)`, `first stage`) %>%
  kbl(caption = "Overlap by propensity-score quintile — Kitagawa spec, somecol (the low-ESS cell)",
      align = c("l","r","r","r","r","r","r")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) %>%
  row_spec(which(ov_kit$`n(Z=0)` < 30), background = "#fdeaea") %>%
  footnote(general = "Highlighted rows: bins with very few Z=0 observations — thin overlap. A near-zero or negative 'first stage' within a bin is where the kappa weights flip sign and explode.")
Overlap by propensity-score quintile — Kitagawa spec, somecol (the low-ESS cell)
bin n n(Z=1) n(Z=0) Pr(D|Z=1) Pr(D|Z=0) first stage
Q1 (low p̂) 623 209 414 0.421 0.331 0.090
Q2 781 438 343 0.543 0.522 0.022
Q3 443 370 73 0.524 0.507 0.017
Q4 1029 912 117 0.605 0.419 0.186
Q5 (high p̂) 134 124 10 0.363 0.200 0.163
Note:
Highlighted rows: bins with very few Z=0 observations — thin overlap. A near-zero or negative ‘first stage’ within a bin is where the kappa weights flip sign and explode.

6. Summary: Three Drivers of the Low ESS

For the meeting. The low ESS in Card is not an error — it is the visible symptom of three reinforcing design features, all absent in the Vietnam application:

  1. Weak first stage (Section 2). College proximity moves treatment far less than the draft lottery moved veteran status, so the conditional first stage in the weight denominator is small for many cells.

  2. Dispersed / boundary propensity scores (Section 3). The richer Card and Kitagawa covariate sets push \(\hat p(X)\) toward 0 and 1, where the \(1/p\) and \(1/(1-p)\) terms blow up — unlike Vietnam’s tightly clustered \(\hat p \approx 0.46\).

  3. Sign-reversing cells & thin overlap (Sections 4–5). A small number of observations with high \(\hat p\) but \(Z=0\) (or low \(\hat p\) but \(Z=1\)) receive enormous weights and carry most of the total — the ESS simply counts how few observations effectively drive the estimate.

The Kitagawa specification is worst on all three counts, which is exactly why its ESS approaches 1.

contrast <- data.frame(
  Feature = c("First-stage F (typical)",
              "p̂(X) standard deviation",
              "Share of p̂ near 0 or 1",
              "Resulting ESS"),
  Vietnam = c("≫ 10 (strong)", "≈ 0.10 (tight)", "≈ 0%", "uniform, moderate"),
  `Card / Kitagawa` = c("can fall below 10", 
                        sprintf("%.3f / %.3f", sd(card$p_card), sd(card$p_kit)),
                        sprintf("%.1f%% / %.1f%%",
                                100*mean(card$p_card<0.05 | card$p_card>0.95),
                                100*mean(card$p_kit<0.05  | card$p_kit>0.95)),
                        "collapses toward 1"),
  check.names = FALSE
)
contrast %>%
  kbl(caption = "Why Card differs from Vietnam — side by side",
      align = c("l","l","l")) %>%
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) %>%
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE) %>%
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE)
Why Card differs from Vietnam — side by side
Feature Vietnam Card / Kitagawa
First-stage F (typical) ≫ 10 (strong) can fall below 10
p̂(X) standard deviation ≈ 0.10 (tight) 0.236 / 0.228
Share of p̂ near 0 or 1 ≈ 0% 0.6% / 0.0%
Resulting ESS uniform, moderate collapses toward 1

Design diagnostics for the Card (1995) application.
All weight/propensity functions sourced from functions_all.R.
